config.dliModeTooltip: Choose how you measure light. PAR sensors measure photosynthetically active radiation directly. Weather stations measure total solar radiation including heat and UV.
config.tBaseHint: Minimum temperature below which the crop stops growing
config.tBaseTooltip: TBase is the starting point of the RTR formula. Below this temperature, the plant ceases to grow. Each crop has a different TBase. You can adjust this to fine-tune your target temperatures.

calculator.title: RTR Calculator
calculator.rtrHint: Higher RTR = more temperature per unit of light
calculator.rtrTooltip: RTR (Radiation–Temperature Ratio) links the 24-hour average temperature to the daily light sum. Light determines how much sugars are produced (source), while temperature determines how much sugars are burnt and distributed (sinks). A higher RTR means a warmer environment relative to light — steering the plant more generatively (more fruiting). A lower RTR keeps the plant more vegetative (more leaf growth).
calculator.dliHintPar: Total photosynthetic light received today (from PAR sensor)
calculator.dliHintRad: Total solar radiation received today (from weather station pyranometer)
calculator.dliTooltip: DLI measures the total amount of light a plant receives in one day — think of it as the plant's daily 'food budget' of light. On a dull winter day this might be 5–10, on a bright summer day 30–50. The more light, the more sugars the plant produces, and the higher temperature it can handle.
calculator.lightPeriodTooltip: The number of hours of daylight today. This determines how the 24-hour average temperature is split between day and night. Longer days mean the day temperature has more weight in the 24-hour average.
calculator.tDayHint: The average temperature inside your greenhouse during daylight hours
calculator.tDayTooltip: This is the average temperature inside your greenhouse during the light period — the micro-climate your plants actually experience, not the outdoor temperature. It is controlled by your heating, ventilation, and cooling systems. Together with the night temperature and the light/dark split, this determines the 24-hour average.
calculator.t24hTooltip: The target average temperature over a full 24-hour period, calculated from your light data and RTR. This is the most important number — it should match the plant's needs based on how much light it received. Too warm relative to light = stretched, weak plants. Too cool = slow, stocky growth.
calculator.darkPeriodTooltip: Hours of darkness = 24h minus the light period. Plants use the dark period to process and transport sugars. A sufficient dark period is important for healthy plant development.
calculator.tNightTooltip: The temperature to maintain inside your greenhouse during the dark period to achieve the target 24-hour average. This is the micro-climate temperature at plant level, not outside air. Plants use the night to process sugars made during the day. Too warm at night wastes energy through excess respiration. Too cold slows sugar transport and growth.
calculator.impossibleNight: Impossible to achieve — adjust day temperature or light period
calculator.heatStressWarning: T24h is outside the accepted range. Consider reducing RTR or increasing ventilation.
calculator.heatStressAlarm: T24h is in the alarm zone! Immediate action needed — increase cooling or shading.
calculator.modeForwardHint: Set your RTR and light data → get the ideal 24-hour temperature and night temperature targets
calculator.modeReverseHint: Enter your actual day and night temperatures → see what RTR you're achieving and whether it's balanced
calculator.tNightInputHint: The average temperature inside your greenhouse during the night
calculator.effectiveRtrTooltip: This is the RTR value that corresponds to your actual temperature and light conditions. Compare it to your crop's recommended RTR — if it's much higher, your greenhouse is too warm for the light available. If it's much lower, you could raise the temperature.
calculator.calculatedT24hTooltip: The 24-hour average temperature calculated from your actual day and night temperatures, weighted by light and dark hours. Compare this to the target from the 'Plan Temperature' mode.
calculator.dliZeroInfo: DLI = 0 means there is no light for photosynthesis. RTR guidance is not applicable without light input.
calculator.reverseRtrUnavailable: Effective RTR cannot be calculated when DLI is zero.
calculator.t24hCapWarning: Calculated T24h exceeds realistic greenhouse conditions (>{{max}}°C). Check your inputs.

table.title: Temperature Heatmap
table.cellAria: Temperature {{value}}{{degrees}} at RTR {{rtr}} and DLI {{dli}}. Zone: {{zone}}.
table.mobileHint: Mobile view focuses on the highlighted RTR row and nearby values.

guide.title: Guided Setup
guide.subtitle: Answer a few questions and we'll calculate the ideal temperature for your greenhouse today.
guide.step1Hint: Each crop has different temperature needs. Selecting a crop sets the recommended base temperature (TBase) and RTR values.
guide.step2Hint: Light measurement tells us how much energy your plants received today. This is needed to calculate the right temperature.
guide.step3Hint: Enter the Daily Light Integral (DLI) — the total light your plants received today. More light means the plant can handle higher temperatures.
guide.step4Hint: The light period determines how the 24-hour temperature is split between day and night.
guide.step5Hint: Enter the average temperature inside your greenhouse during daylight hours — this is the micro-climate your plants experience, not the outdoor air temperature.
guide.step6Hint: Based on your inputs, here are your ideal temperature targets for today.
guide.locationSkipHint: Location data has filled in light, daylight hours, and temperature. You can skip ahead or review each step.
guide.noSensor: I don't have a sensor — estimate for me
guide.estimateParModeNote: Estimation mode calculates DLI in PAR units (mol/m²/day).
guide.estimateDisclaimer: Country values are monthly outdoor averages and can vary by local climate, dust, and cloudiness.

crops.title: Crop Profiles
crops.subtitle: Select a crop to see recommended settings based on Plant Empowerment principles. You can always fine-tune TBase and RTR after applying a profile.

home.hero: What temperature should your greenhouse be today?
home.heroSub: Use science-based RTR calculations from Plant Empowerment to find the perfect 24-hour temperature balance for your crops.
home.step1Desc: Input today's daily light integral from your sensor or estimate it based on your region.
home.step2Desc: Choose your crop for optimized temperature recommendations based on Plant Empowerment principles.
home.step3Desc: Receive your ideal 24-hour and night temperature targets to balance plant growth.
home.guidedStart: New here? Try the Guided Setup
home.learnMoreSub: Understanding the science behind the RTR Calculator

education.whatIsRtrText: RTR (Radiation–Temperature Ratio) is a key concept from Plant Empowerment. It links the ideal 24-hour average temperature to the daily light sum. On a dull day plants prefer a lower temperature (e.g. 18°C for tomatoes), while on a bright day they thrive at 24°C or more. A higher RTR leads to warmer, more generative (fruiting) plants. A lower RTR keeps growth more vegetative (leafy). The art of growing is striking the right balance.
education.whatIsDliText: DLI (Daily Light Integral) measures the total photosynthetic light a plant receives in one day — think of it as the plant's daily 'food budget'. It can be measured by a PAR sensor in mol/m²/day, or from a weather station pyranometer in J/cm²/day. A sunny summer day might be 30–50 mol/m²/day, while a cloudy winter day could be just 5–10.
education.whatIsTBaseText: TBase is the minimum temperature below which a crop stops growing. Each crop is different — tomatoes have a TBase around 17°C, while lettuce can grow down to 12°C. In the RTR formula, TBase is the starting point: T24h = TBase + (RTR × DLI) / K. You can fine-tune TBase to steer your plant balance.
education.whyNightTempText: Light determines how much sugars are produced during the day (the source). Temperature determines how much sugars are burnt through respiration and how remaining sugars are distributed to growing parts (the sinks). At night, plants process and transport these sugars. Too warm at night wastes energy. Too cold slows transport. The right night temperature completes the balance.
education.whatIsPlantEmpowermentText: Plant Empowerment is a greenhouse cultivation methodology developed in the Netherlands, also known as 'Het Nieuwe Telen' (HNT) — the New Way of Growing. It focuses on three balances: the energy balance, the water balance, and the assimilate (sugar) balance. The RTR graph is a key tool for monitoring and optimizing the temperature–light balance.

location.title: Auto from Location
location.hint: Fetch today's light data, sunrise/sunset, and temperature from the nearest weather station based on your location.
location.permissionDenied: Location permission denied. Please allow location access or search for your city instead.
location.gpsUnavailable: GPS is unavailable. Try searching for your city instead.
location.networkError: Network error. Please check your connection and try again.
location.fetchFailed: Failed to fetch weather data for this location.
location.noResults: No locations found. Try a different search term.
location.searchFailed: Search failed. Please check your internet connection.
location.invalidCoords: Invalid coordinates. Latitude must be -90 to 90, longitude -180 to 180.
location.dataSource: Weather data from Open-Meteo (nearest station). DLI, light period, and day temperature have been auto-filled.
